does: This paper investigates whether making routing decisions explicit and visible in neural networks actually enables mechanistic interpretation. The authors introduce Block Attention Residuals, which replace fixed residual connections with learned softmax distributions over earlier layer representations, creating an inspectable routing tensor in the forward pass. They conduct a causal intervention study comparing two 0.6B parameter models: a vanilla Qwen3 baseline constrained to follow a deterministic recency-bias routing schedule, and a Block AttnRes variant trained from scratch with learned routing. Using routing-ablation interventions, they probe what routing patterns emerge and whether these patterns reveal interpretable information flow structures. The wrapped baseline exhibits content-independent routing matching its schedule's analytical predictions, while the trained AttnRes model develops three localized routing motifs involving embedding pathways through early MLPs, current-state pathways through early attention/MLPs, and history pathways through late layers.
connects: This work extends recent efforts to improve neural network interpretability by making latent mechanisms directly observable rather than inferred post-hoc. It builds on research into residual connections and information flow in transformers, as well as mechanistic interpretability approaches that use causal intervention to understand model computations. The paper responds to growing interest in learned routing and dynamic computation paths, building specifically on the Block Attention Residuals architecture. It connects to prior work on probing techniques and causal abstraction that seek to identify whether exposed model components actually correspond to interpretable functional units. The comparison between deterministically scheduled routing and learned routing grounds the investigation in concrete architectural differences.
enables: This work opens questions about the gap between observability and interpretability in neural networks. By showing that explicit routing tensors do correlate with interpretable information flow patterns but may require careful analysis to extract meaning, it sets up future work on designing architectures that are both transparent and mechanistically interpretable. The routing motifs identified suggest potential for further investigation into functional specialization across layers and depth, and could inform the design of models with more deliberately structured information paths. The methodology of comparing constrained versus learned routing provides a template for investigating whether architectural transparency enables human understanding, potentially guiding development of inherently interpretable neural architectures and informing debates about when mechanistic interpretability becomes practically achievable.
